metadata.title: Double ML: Partialling Out Confounders with Two Stages

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Aloha, Liam — in for Bear. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: Build it with a CLI, then take it apart?
props.segment: Double ML: Partialling Out Confounders with

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: OLS gives you a biased causal estimate when confounders affect both treatment and outcome. Double ML partials them out with two ML stages, then estimates on clean residuals.
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'double ML — two ML stages, one honest estimate'; OLS bias vs DML precision

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude. Implement Double ML from scratch: two RandomForest residualization stages, then OLS on clean residuals. Compare to naive OLS and print the confidence interval.

props.command: claude "Implement Double ML (Chernozhukov 2018) from scratch. Synthetic data: 10 confounders X, treatment D = 0.5*X1 + X2 + noise, outcome Y = 1.5*D + 0.8*X1 - 0.6*X3 + noise (true effect=1.5). Step 1: RF predict Y from X, get residuals. Step 2: RF predict D from X, get residuals. Step 3: OLS on residuals. Print DML estimate, OLS estimate, truth, 95% CI. No econml."

beats.narration_text: Read it. The double means two ML stages — one for the outcome, one for the treatment. The key insight: ML models absorb nonlinear confounding that OLS misses, but you regress residuals linearly to get interpretable causal estimates.

props.code:
# double_ml.py — Claude Code output

# Stage 1: residualize Y on X
rf_y = RandomForest().fit(X, Y)
Y_resid = Y - rf_y.predict(X)

# Stage 2: residualize D on X
rf_d = RandomForest().fit(X, D)
D_resid = D - rf_d.predict(X)

# Stage 3: OLS on clean residuals
# theta = Cov(D_resid, Y_resid) / Var(D_resid)
# This removes confounding absorbed by both RF models
theta_dml = np.cov(D_resid, Y_resid)[0,1] / np.var(D_resid)

beats.narration_text: The number line shows OLS estimate at 1.95 — biased upward by 0.45 because confounders inflate the coefficient. DML lands at 1.48 — within 0.02 of the truth at 1.50.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: DML pipeline diagram + number line comparing OLS/DML/truth

beats.narration_text: Now ask Claude to add cross-fitting — split the sample into folds so the residuals come from out-of-fold predictions. This is the variance reduction step Chernozhukov requires.

props.command:
claude "update double_ml.py:
  -> add 5-fold cross-fitting (residuals from out-of-fold predictions)
  -> compute standard error using Neyman orthogonality formula
  -> compare cross-fit CI width to naive DML CI width"

beats.narration_text: Cross-fitting narrows the confidence interval by 20 percent and removes the overfitting bias from the residualization step. The Neyman orthogonality condition is what makes this work — the moment equation is insensitive to first-stage estimation error.
beats.visual_intent: slate: cross-fit vs naive DML confidence intervals; standard error comparison

beats.narration_text: Double ML works because of Neyman orthogonality — the causal parameter is the one that survives after you partial out everything else. Two ML stages absorb nonlinear confounding that OLS leaves on the table.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: two stages, Neyman orthogonality, residual regression

beats.narration_text: Your move: take your highest-stakes OLS regression and run DML beside it. If the estimates diverge by more than one standard error, your confounders are nonlinear and OLS is giving you the wrong answer.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: run DML alongside OLS, check divergence, proceed to HTE reel

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. Double ML works because of Neyman orthogonality — the causal parameter is the one that survives after you partial out everything else. Two ML stages absorb nonlinear confounding that OLS leaves on the table. Your move: take your highest-stakes OLS regression and run DML beside it. If the estimates diverge by more than one standard error, your confounders are nonlinear and OLS is giving you the wrong answer.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Double ML: Partialling Out Confounders with Two Stages] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
